title: July 9, 1979: Voyager 2’s Jupiter visit

whyMatters: Noticia de actualidad en el sector de ciencia.

multimedia.alt: A digital illustration shows a large, tan- and cream-banded planet on the left and a dark spacecraft with a prominent white dish antenna positioned to its right. Long, thin booms and antennas extend from the spacecraft, crossing in front of the planet and out toward the sides of the frame where several small, textured moons are scattered. The entire scene is set against a dark background dense with countless tiny, faint stars.
multimedia.caption: A digital illustration shows a large, tan- and cream-banded planet on the left and a dark spacecraft with a prominent white dish antenna positioned to its right. Long, thin booms and antennas extend from the spacecraft, crossing in front of the planet and out toward the sides of the frame where several small, textured moons are scattered. The entire scene is set against a dark background dense with countless tiny, faint stars.

fullArticle:
On July 9, 1979, NASA’s Voyager 2 spacecraft came within 404,003 miles (650,180 kilometers) of Jupiter. Launched in August 1977, the Voyager probes were originally planned to study Jupiter and Saturn. Voyager 1 arrived at Jupiter in March 1979, and Voyager 2 followed a few months later.

Equipped with narrow- and wide-angle cameras, an interferometer, an ultraviolet spectrometer, a magnetometer, and more, Voyager 2 showed that the Great Red Spot was a storm system and revealed Jupiter’s thin ring system. Its investigations of the gas giant’s moons included confirming active volcanism on the moon Io and taking high-resolution images of Europa that suggested a ocean beneath its icy crust. Its success allowed Voyager 2 to extend its mission, becoming the only spacecraft to perform close-up ex...
